food_subgroup: Sichuan sesame-chilli noodle bowl with minced pork; 1 bowl approximately 380g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Dan Dan Noodles as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be a wheat noodle dish node; not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat noodle dish with chilli sauce (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0L3A used as closest parent for a wheat-based noodle dish with chilli sauce. No exact FoodEx2 entry for Sichuan Dan Dan Noodles exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dan Dan Noodles as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: topped with preserved Sichuan vegetable (ya cai)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Dan Dan Noodles weighs approximately 380g and provides around 550 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Dan Dan Noodles — Component Calculation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for wheat noodles, ground pork, sesame paste, and condiment components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients (wheat noodles, ground pork, sesame paste/tahini, soy sauce, chilli oil, peanuts, spring onion). No single USDA entry exists for Dan Dan Noodles as a composite dish. Values represent a weighted average per 100g of the assembled 380g bowl. Sodium reflects the compounding contribution of doubanjiang (~400mg), ya cai (~400mg), and soy seasoning (~400mg) per bowl, yielding approximately 329mg per 100g (1,250mg per 380g serving).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat noodles and analogous noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; fat-moderated GI estimation for sesame paste noodle preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 estimated using wheat noodle GI reference values from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain wheat noodles GI ~52–65). The sesame paste (zhima jiang) fat content significantly moderates gastric emptying and starch digestion rate, reducing the effective GI from the plain noodle baseline (~65) to approximately 58. GL per 380g bowl calculated as (58 × 55g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 31.9, rounded to 30.7 per input source. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dan Dan Noodles exists in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1,250mg per bowl, approximately 329mg per 100g) from compounding fermented ingredients: doubanjiang, ya cai, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Dan Dan Noodles due to ~1,250mg sodium per bowl (52–63% of the 2,000mg daily limit); if consumed, request reduced ya cai and sauce to lower sodium by up to 700mg per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~58) with high glycemic load (~31 per bowl) driven by ~55g available carbohydrate from wheat noodles, partially offset by sesame paste fat slowing digestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Dan Dan Noodles as an occasional noodle choice (GL ~31 per bowl is high); pairing with extra minced pork to increase protein and requesting smaller noodle portions can reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1,250mg per bowl) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate (~5.7g per bowl from pork and sesame paste); sesame paste provides beneficial unsaturated fats and lignans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Dan Dan Noodles to occasional consumption due to high sodium load; the sesame paste contributes beneficial unsaturated fatty acids and anti-inflammatory lignans (sesamin, sesamolin), but the sodium burden from doubanjiang and ya cai outweighs these benefits for regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium from fermented ingredient stacking (doubanjiang + ya cai) is the primary concern; minor anti-inflammatory contributions from sesame lignans, garlic quercetin, and ginger gingerol
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should limit Dan Dan Noodles due to high sodium (1,250mg per bowl); requesting no ya cai reduces sodium by approximately 700mg and makes the dish more suitable for occasional consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dan Dan Noodles (担担面, dàn dàn miàn) is a classic Sichuan street food originating in Chengdu, China, named after the bamboo shoulder poles (扁担, biǎndàn) used by street vendors to carry the sauce and noodles. A standard restaurant bowl (~380g) contains thin wheat noodles dressed in a rich sesame paste (zhima jiang) and chilli oil sauce, topped with stir-fried minced pork, preserved Sichuan vegetable (ya cai), spring onion, crushed peanuts, and Sichuan peppercorn. The flavour profile is simultaneously spicy, numbing (má là), nutty, savoury, and slightly sweet. Per 100g: ~145 kcal, 5.3g protein, 7.1g fat, 14.5g carbohydrate, 329mg sodium. Per bowl (~380g): ~550 kcal, 20g protein, 27g fat, 55g carbohydrate, ~1,250mg sodium. The sesame paste is the caloric centrepiece, contributing the majority of fat and a significant portion of calories. Sodium is critically high due to the compounding of three fermented/salted ingredients: doubanjiang (~400mg), ya cai (~400mg), and soy seasoning (~400mg) per bowl. GI ~58 (medium), GL ~31 per bowl — the sesame paste fat moderates the GI compared to plain wheat noodle soups. Clinically: high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium; moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to high GL. Allergens: gluten, sesame, peanuts, soy. Dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data rather than direct laboratory analysis of the composite dish. No single approved-source entry exists for Dan Dan Noodles as a prepared dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodle reference values from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for sesame paste fat content. Sodium estimate reflects typical restaurant preparation with standard quantities of doubanjiang, ya cai, and soy sauce.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Dan Dan Noodles dish (sesame paste + pork fat blend) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central; individual component fatty acid data could be used for a future refined calculation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled dish is not reported in the USDA component sources used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy data for wheat noodles and pork mince at typical serving quantities. This is a Chinese Sichuan dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and have been set to null. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. FoodEx2 code A0L3A is used as the closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Dan Dan Noodles exists. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dan Dan Noodles appears in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date.
